Just east of San Francisco.
Yeah?
Yeah.

Uh-huh.
For sure.
Yes.

That's great.

I did for the next chapter of my life, but good to meet you.

I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX, so I work hand-in-hand with our CEO, Marcel, and typically, you're working with me on the first and second call to really scope out what a partnership could look like, and then I can obviously bring in Marcel as early as it makes sense.
Okay, excellent.
Would love just maybe a little intro and context from you.
I know that, you know, the team was like curious about how GrowthX might be able to help, so anything that you're thinking about there before we jump in?
Yeah, absolutely.
You want some background on Mutiny?
I know Mutiny pretty well.
I used to work at Seros and feel like I was aware of all the different content creation tools, and you guys are awesome for ABM, so definitely know of Mutiny from...
the time there.
Okay, great.
So what context would be helpful as we start the conversation, just so I, like, answer your question properly?
Yeah, definitely.
I would just say...

Like, your interest in growthx in terms of how we might be able to partner, what you're thinking about in terms of, like, optimizing for AEO and GEO, just anything you think might be relevant prior to me going into, like, an overview of what we do and digging deeper into, like, what the partnership could look like.
Perfect.
Okay, yeah, I can give you that context.
So I lead the marketing team here at the moment.
We are hiring a full-time head of marketing.
I'm the interim head of marketing, but I've been with the team since December.
We have invested somewhat significantly in SEO work in the last two, three months where we are doing a lot more blog content.
We started investing in Reddit and, like, actually working with an agency to get us more kind of exposure on Reddit.
It's kind of gray hat work that we're doing over there, but it does actually drive results for us where they're...
Kind of posting in B2B marketing channels and so on about ABM tools and then Mutiny somehow shows up in the comments section.
I don't know how that happens, but somehow we end up in those conversations as a result of that partnership.
And so that is actually starting to pay off.
What we want to do is really be the definitive source for ABM-specific content.
I think our challenge right now is that our website traffic is declining because more people are going to LLMs for information.
They're less likely to go to websites in general.
And so we're trying to play this game of like, how do we ensure that we maintain our presence?
And how do we make sure that our investment in content actually helps us to rank?
So those are open questions we're trying to learn.
And so we wanted to kind of hear from you guys in terms of how you partner and what you do.
Make sure we're using the best practices.
Make sure we're working with the smartest people in the space because I feel like it's kind of the wild, wild west.
We're all learning again.
Yeah, 100%.
That's super helpful context.
The partner that's working with you on Reddit Exposure, are they also creating blog content for you guys today or doing anything around SEO strategy?
No, we're working with two different teams.
we're working with a team called Impact Partners specifically on Reddit.
That's a very small monthly retainer.
That team is, I think they're based in, honestly, in like South Africa or something.
But we have a very small retainer with them on a monthly basis where they guarantee us a certain number of, like, comments on industry-specific subreddits.
We are working with another team called Omniscient on our SEO strategy.
They do backlinking for us and they build content briefs for us.
And then I actually have my own internal team of freelance writers who are producing that content and we're publishing that content on a recurring basis.
So we're working with two different teams right now.
Okay, awesome.
And then obviously you want to, like, improve site traffic, improve, like, where you're showing up in AI search.
Would you say that it's more of like a scale challenge to solve for?
Like how can you scale quality?
Or is it more about like improving the quality of the amount of content that you're producing currently?
Are you looking to scale content creation or maintain the current quantity?
I don't think scalings are an issue.
Like I can publish four to six blogs a week right now with our current system.
It's more that the pool of search terms to me feels limited.
Because like ultimately we're trying to reach like a demand gen ABM practitioner.
And I have gone and spent many hours with our SEO agency.
I'm like, how many articles can we possibly write on this topic before we've just kind of saturated what's possible?
And there's not a ton of volume on these terms.
So I'm like, are we doing the right work?
Is it getting us the total addressable market of people who are looking for this?
Is there a missing piece of it for LLM that we're not currently investing in?
Really hard for us to know.
Like everybody, I've talked to a bunch of different teams.
Everyone says that they can help with LLM, but like, it's really, like, how do I really know, right?
So it's kind of hard to know what's possible.
But yeah, I mean, I just want to show up for people who are looking for ABM best practices, for people who are ABM practitioners who are looking for high quality content.
Like, I want Mutiny to be there, the de facto definitive content team.
So it's not really about quantity of content, because I think we can do that, and we've proven we can do that.
It's more like, are we building the right quality of content, and are we showing up as much as possible?
Okay, perfect.
No, thanks for all the context.
That's super helpful.
I would say, like, high level, what we're working on with clients is typically scaling out their editorial content to where they're publishing daily.
right.

Then layering on programmatic SEO where there is like a clearly defined use case.
So those are the work streams we're typically executing on.

Unfortunately, it's no longer, you know, jumping into Google and typing in like software for XYZ.
And so like the ability to scale quality is like the number one reason I would say folks have been hiring growthx.
And so I think that, you know, that's one thing if you don't think scaling would be very helpful, that could, you know, just be a sign that like maybe we're not the right fit.
But obviously we can go much deeper on like the SEO opportunity and look into.
To like the keywords more granularly as well.
biggest bottleneck for scaling is our CMS.

We Contentful and it's like a really, it's really painful for us to publish blogs.

It's like ends up being the number one reason why we can't publish more quickly.
Because everything else is like fairly straightforward, actually.

Or are they doing that instead?
Because I know that's like a very tactical question.
But for us, like it is a massive bottleneck for us because of the Contentful implementation.
And like no one knows that tool.
We barely know the tool.
So it's a big challenge for us.
How do you guys typically handle that?
Yeah, that's one advantage.
We do all the staging and publishing if our clients give access to the CMS.

And it's this combination, right, of, like, AI workflows with experts in the loop.
And so, you know, the team that you'd be working with at GrowthX, we've got our own internal platform where we do all of the execution.
And then we have a managing director, managing an editor, and content manager who works on your account.
And these folks, like, all have SEO backgrounds or content marketing backgrounds and have worked at, like, some of the logos that you see here.
Our chief content officer who oversees the whole operation, he was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch for over a decade.
And so this is a little bit behind, like, our approach is we can be end-to-end strategy, daily publishing, last-mile execution, LLM monitoring and analytics.
And we can have, like, the human touch in addition to the workflows.
And you're CMS agnostic?
CMS agnostic, there are definitely preferences, but I would say, like, contentful, any headless...
CMS is one that we're comfortable with.
Okay.
But I guess, do you know much about like the origin story of GrowthX or like our CEO, Marcel?
Because that speaks to a little bit of like how we're different as like an agency partner.
Yeah, for sure.
So with Marcel, I mean, he's always believed like brands should be publishers and never spent much on paid throughout his career.

Whether it was like his first job overseeing content marketing at IBM, where they built this website called securityintelligence.com, or whether it was his last two jobs before GrowthX, the CMO at Scale through the acquisition, and then the CMO at DeepGram, where he replicated, right, like the process of building out an editorial content engine using AI workflows for generating a really robust brief, doing deep research and fact-checking, you know, it's really...
Like, Marcel built GrowthX as the product he always wanted as a CML.
And I would say that, you know, our approach is we make sure that for every piece of content that we create, we take it through the most important factors for GEO.
And obviously, like, you can summarize that as credible, comprehensive, and current.

But there's just a lot that goes in, as I show you, like, a piece of content getting created that does, like, much more than someone using, like, Claude or ChatGPT to try to speed up the process, right, of creating the content.
And so, you know, our approach at GrowthX here is that we split it up into two phases.
The first phase is a strategy sprint, where we develop these rich artifacts around your ICP, your company, your brand voice, and it gives the context necessary for our workflows to be hyper-relevant.
And then in phase two, we're actually going in and executing on that roadmap of assignments that we've created.
And all of the assignments are concentrated in, like, these different topic clusters.
And so the idea is that, you know, we're very audience first in terms of our strategy of who we're creating content for.
And then we want to create content that represents the topics where you want to be the authority or the answer, right, to those questions that your ICP might have.
So it's very, like, logical in terms of how we want to approach it.
But then we layer in, right, all the search volume, the keyword difficulty, the competitor content to inform, like, which assignments we actually prioritize.
Yeah.
Yeah, we've invested in this strategy work with another SEO agency somewhat recently.
So I think it's kind of like why, for me, it's like, you know, there's like a switching cost, but then also this fixed fee that we've been asked, we've paid for in the past.
And it was helpful, but again, like, my conclusion, and this is where I'm like, if I had another conversation.
I sure met with Marcel, like what I really want to understand is like, how much juice is there to squeeze in this very narrow area that we work in?
Because I've done SEO and like LLM work with other teams like healthcare and other verticals where I'm like, there's a lot of search volume, there's a lot of topics, there's a lot of areas.
I just think that like after doing this work with a different agency for the last four months, I'm like, gosh, like I don't know how many actual potential impressions there are on this topic.
Like it's just seems like a narrow audience.
Like, are there certain verticals or teams where there's kind of diminishing returns or like a limit by which you can build this content on a recurring basis?
Because I think five articles per week, we've kind of been doing that.
And I'm like, I look at the content calendar and I'm like, by next month, we've kind of saturated these topics.
Yeah.
Then what?
What do we do next?
I don't know.
It's such a good question.
And I definitely think like.
Like.
And talking about it with Marcel, potentially setting up a follow-up where Marcel can give more of that analytics, right, behind, like, your opportunity in particular, like, would be very helpful.
And all I can do is really just, like, show you examples of how you've been able to continue to squeeze.
Yeah, I would love to just, if you have some case studies, I'd love to kind of, if there's, like, an example of a client's success story in this deck, like, let's walk through that.
That would be helpful for me.
Yeah, definitely.
Because I was kind of, like, taking a look at, you know, the different pages that y'all have created.
And it definitely seems like there's, like, more blog content that you could create that would add value.

But a couple, like, success stories.

And so you can see that we like took a bunch of quotes from town and then found the right places to weave those quotes into the articles that we were working on.

So this is just like an example of how we can get really technical or we can get really specific where there's like a higher bar for accuracy and still accommodate, right, like what the customer might need.
In terms of like an ROI story, I think Augment Code's a great example.
The Augment Code, AI coding, they compete with Cursor, GitHub, Copilot.

It's three.

And then for like a piece of, you know, their product that's a bit differentiated, they can.

For those guides, can you go back to their website and the guides themselves?
Can you click on one?

And it can be reviewed by someone from your creative team.
And so we create cover images for pretty much every client that we work with.

Pretty cool.

did artifacts across all their target industry verticals.
